CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moving into Arizona and a cold easterly flow into the into the Little Colorado River Valley, White Mountains, and Eastern Mogollon Rim caused widespread freezing drizzle and freezing rain. Law enforcement officers and the general public reported 47 wrecks and roll overs between Winslow and the New Mexico State Line and south to Show Low. Highway 77 between Holbrook and Show Low was close due to the ice build up on the road.  At 900 AM, all law enforcement and emergency personnel on duty in Navajo Country were committed.  Five people died and many others were injured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility due to blowing dust, also described as a temporary blackout, contributed to two traffic pileups on a stretch of Interstate 8 west of Casa Grande.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February was as the sixth driest February on record in Flagstaff since 1898 with only 0.09" of precipitation measured. No snowfall was recorded at the Flagstaff airport. The average precipitation for the month is 2.56 inches of precipitation. The average snowfall is 20.8 inches. Flagstaff received only 2.58 inches of precipitation (including melted snow) from September 1st 2005 through February 28th 2006. This is only 21 percent of average precipitation and is least amount of precipitation ever recorded. Average precipitation is 12.48 inches during this period.  Last year...23.81 inches of precipitation had occurred during this same period.  Dry conditions existed across all of northern Arizona with many locations recording 15 to 40 percent of their normal precipitation for the fall and winter months so far.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced strong and damaging winds across the Little Colorado River Valley.  Some of the high winds reports include: Winslow 56 MPH MS/68 MPH MG, St. Johns 40 MPH MS/56 MPH MG, Show Low Airport 49 MPH MS/74 MPH MG, and Window Rock Airport 44 MPH MS/ 56 MPH MG. Spotters in Winslow reported sheet metal roofing blowing around, numerous tree limbs blown down, and shingles blowing off roofs. A spotter in Tuba City reported metal blowing off roof tops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the west coast of Baja California brought moisture north from the Gulf of Mexico into Southeastern Arizona. This moisture allowed for isolated showers and thunderstorms to develop in Cochise County. Several trained spotters reported that a severe thunderstorm produced three quarter inch hail.

Power to a number of communities was knocked out as heavy snow broke tree limbs and took out power lines. At one point, 20,000 APS customers were without power, mainly affecting Globe, Miami, and Superior. Numerous trees and branches were down at the Boyce Thompson Arboretum near Superior. Unusually heavy snow was reported from observers in areas to the north and east of the Phoenix metro area, with 10 inches on the ground at Punkin Center.  Heavy rainfall also occurred at Queen Creek, where one gauge recorded 3.39 inches up to 9 pm Saturday.  This event also ended the 143-day record long streak of days without any measurable rain in Phoenix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected all of Northern Arizona from Friday (03/10) though most of the day on Sunday (3/12). Heavy snowfall and rare low elevation snowfall occurred over almost all of Northern Arizona. Snow levels dropped to as low as 2000 feet south of the Mogollon Rim. Strong winds accompanied this storm...leading to significant blowing and drifting snow. This made for difficult driving conditions on snow packed and icy roads with some areas having very poor visibility. Some storm totals from across northern Arizona (in inches) include: Alpine 24, Ash Fork 7, Bagdad 5, Black Canyon City and Camp Verde T, Chinle 3, Clarkdale 0.5, Concho 23, Cordes Junction 6, Christopher Creek 37, Crown King 16, Flagstaff 30, Forest Lakes 40-48, Fredonia 6, Ganado 6-8, Grand Canyon - N. A strong and cold Pacific storm system moved down the west coast and into Southern Arizona, bringing the first significant winter storm to the Desert Southwest.  As the system moved through Southeast Arizona it brought significant snowfall to the mountains as well as some much needed precipitation to the valley's.  Reports of a quarter inch to over an inch of rainfall were reported throughout Southeast Arizona, with the Tucson International Airport reporting 0.22 inch for storm total rainfall.  Most other locations reported more than a quarter inch of rainfall with several stations reporting over an inch. Many locations reported snowfall, with only two stations reporting heavy snow criteria amounts. Hannagan Meadow Lodge (9100 feet) in Greenlee county reported a storm total snowfall amount of 24 inches while Mt. Lemmon Ski Lodge (8200 feet) in Eastern Pima County reported 18 inches of storm total snowfall.  Several lower elevations also reported some snowfall with the Town of Vail (3500 feet) receiving 3 inches, the City of Oracle (4500 feet) receiving 3 inches and Kitt Peak Observatory (7000 feet) reporting 8 inches.  Snow levels from the Tucson area and south dropped quite low, with the lowest accumulation appearing to be near 3500 feet.

The February Fire was caused by an abandoned campfire along the Mogollon Rim about 12 miles north of Payson. Persistant northeast winds pushed the fire south and down off the Rim.  The fire grew to 4,200 acres before it was contained.  This area would usually have over a foot of snow on the ground in early February.  The very dry winter contributed to the rapid spread of this fire.  One snowstorm dumped over 3 feet of snow on the burned area about 3 weeks later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, estimated at over 45 mph, resulted in very low visibility with dense blowing dust and blowing sand. One spotter reported only 1/4 mile visibility along I-8 near Gila Bend.

Winds in excess of 35 mph were reported in many areas. These winds, together with very dry soil conditions, combined to result in dense blowing dust and blowing sand, which cut the visibility to less than 1/4 mile in some areas. One of the strongest wind speeds was measured at Chandler Municipal airport where a peak gust of 56 mph occurred just before 6 pm MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility reduced to less than a quarter mile in blowing dust just south of Coolidge and in Arizona City.

Strong thunderstorms east of Phoenix generated strong and gusty winds which resulted in areas of blowing dust and sand. Many areas of the East Valley experienced visibilities less than 1/4 mile. Winds were clocked at over 40 mph in many areas, with trained spotters estimating winds up to 50-55 mph.

Winds increased throughout the Phoenix metropolitan area during the late morning hours. Luke AFB recorded a peak gust of 48 mph and low visibility due to blowing dust at 1108 mst.  Winds also brought down several tree branches at about the same time  in nearby Waddell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leading edge of a very strong winter storm brought high winds to the Little Colorado River Valley during the day. Strong wind blew a metal roof off of a shed in Cornville.  Petrified Forest National Park reported sustained wind between 35 and 40 MPH with gusts to 60 MPH.

The leading edge of a very strong winter storm brought high winds to the Little Colorado River Valley during the day. Strong wind blew a metal roof off of a shed in Cornville.  Petrified Forest National Park reported sustained wind between 35 and 40 MPH with gusts to 60 MPH.

A late season cold front moved into the Great Basin during the day bringing windy conditions across northern Arizona.  The highest winds were reported in the Eastern Mogollon Rim and the White Mountains.  National Forest Service lookout towers reported sustained winds between 45 and 55 MPH with gusts between 80 and 90 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two drownings at Lake Mead National Recreation Area.  32YO female fell off a personal watercraft near Princess Cove, AZ on Lake Mohave, and 21YO male fell off a small boat at an unknown location.  Strong winds contributed to both deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed over the city of Tucson knocking down and damaging power poles in three separate locations.  The storm also produced blowing dust reducing visibility to near zero in many locations.  Approximately 30 power poles were damaged or snapped causing more than 24,000 customers to lose power.  Fortunately, there were no major injuries reported.

A late season cold front moved into the Great Basin during the day bringing windy conditions across northern Arizona.  The highest winds were reported in the Eastern Mogollon Rim and the White Mountains.  National Forest Service lookout towers reported sustained winds between 45 and 55 MPH with gusts between 80 and 90 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two drownings at Lake Mead National Recreation Area.  69YO female fell off a personal watercraft near Temple Bar, and 4YO female fell off a raft near South Telephone Cove.  Strong winds contributed to both drownings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms marched across the greater Tucson metropolitan area knocking down power poles, producing golf ball sized hail and creating blowing dust.  Approximately 10 power poles fell in 5 different locations damaging two vehicles and closing a portion of Interstate 10.  Neither the large hail nor the reduced visibility from blowing dust sparked any reports of injury or damage.
A thunderstorm outflow caused power lines to be knocked down leaving the entire town of Ajo without power.
A thunderstorm outflow caused power lines to be knocked down leaving about 2600 homes without power.

Interstate 10 near the Arizona-New Mexico border was closed for approximately two hours due to strong wind and reduced visibility in blowing dust.  Also, an injury (indirect) accident occurred 20 miles east of Willcox, AZ.
A thunderstorm outflow caused damage to paneling of a mobile and adjacent fencing.

The La Barranca Fire started Thursday, June 1, 2006 at around 1:50 PM about 3 miles east of the Village of Oak Creek. The fire was human caused by a fence company that was grinding on a metal fence post on private property.  The fire burned 836 acres through grass, brush, and pinyon-juniper woodland. The estimated fire fighting cost was $1,200,000. The damage included: 1 primary residence lost, 1 partially-damaged residence, 1 guest house/shop destroyed, 3 outbuildings destroyed.  There were 158 structures saved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen near Leupp.
A second funnel cloud was seen near the town of Leupp.
One inch diameter hail fell in Keams Canyon.
A thunderstorm with microburst hit the Williams Airport with 72KT wind gusts.
A thunderstorm produced wind gusts to 59 KTS (68 MPH) in Chino Valley.
A thunderstorm gust front produced a 58 MPH wind gust at the Winslow Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Woody Fire was caused by a moving car trailer with a flat tire at about 4 PM. The fire started along I-40 about 3/4 of a mile west of Woody Mountain Road on the western edge of Flagstaff.  There was a Red Flag Warning in effect at the time.  The fire quickly spread through about 150 acres of pine forest.  No homes or structures were damaged by the fire.  Several subdivisions were threatened and evacuated. Several power poles were burned which caused power outages and damage to the local electrical substation.  Power went out for 1,200 customers for the rest of the night. The fire stopped spreading by about 1000 PM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with wind gusts between 55 and 60 MPH hit Black Canyon City.  The thunderstorm knocked down a power pole.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust storm reduced the visibility to 150 meters on I-40 between exit 260 and 270 for about half an hour.
A funnel cloud was reported by Navajo County Police near Jack Rabbit along I-40 (Exit 257).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry microburst caused 7 power poles to snap and damaged 2 others forcing a dozen homes to be evacuated as power lines draped across them.  No one was injured and there was minimal damage to the homes.

Two separate traffic accidents occurred during a thunderstorm induced dust storm. The westbound lanes of Interstate 10 were closed at the Trico-Marana Road exit due to a fatal collision (indirect).  Meanwhile, another non-major injury collision near Red Rock caused the eastbound lanes of Interstate 10 to be closed.

Two separate traffic accidents occurred during a thunderstorm induced dust storm. The westbound lanes of Interstate 10 were closed at the Trico-Marana Road exit due to a fatal collision (indirect).  Meanwhile, another non-major injury collision near Red Rock caused the eastbound lanes of Interstate 10 to be closed.

Two separate traffic accidents occurred during a thunderstorm induced dust storm.  Near mile post 363 along westbound Interstate 10, close to Bowie, AZ, a multiple vehicle pileup left 2 people dead (indirect) and at least 7 injured (indirect).  At the same time in the same area along eastbound Interstate 10 another collision occurred leaving at least two people injured (indirect).
Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused La Terraza Wash to quickly flood forcing rip-rap into the roadway.  A trained weather spotter measured 1.76 inches of rain in less than one and one half hours.
Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused Moson Road to quickly flood forcing officials to close the road.

A series of thunderstorms marched across the greater Tucson metropolitan area knocking down power poles, producing golf ball sized hail and creating blowing dust.  Approximately 10 power poles fell in 5 different locations damaging two vehicles and closing a portion of Interstate 10.  Neither the large hail nor the reduced visibility from blowing dust sparked any reports of injury or damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: With higher gusts.  One shelter destroyed, and one large tree blown down.
Also blowing dust and sand.
Flash flooding in the Peacock Mountains.

Maricopa County officials reported Bartlett Lake Road washed out at Camp Creek and mile post 4. Some of the heavy rainfall amounts included 2.64 inches at Camp Creek, with 2.43 inches falling between 1115 pm and 1215 am Sunday July 30th. Another site, Seven Springs Wash had a storm total of 2.2 inches with 1.76 inches falling between 11 pm and midnight. The road to Horsehoe Dam was closed for several days because of the heavy rainfall.
Thunderstorm winds blew a sign into a pickup truck.
Winds estimated at 45 mph caused widespread blowing dust with visibility less than 1/4 mile in Casa Grande and Apache Junction.
High winds resulted in areas of dense blowing dust. Visibility was reduced to less than a quarter mile in many areas of Phoenix and the East Valley.
Roof damage reported in the Tierra Grande subdivision.
Several power poles brought down by high winds.

Winds associated with thunderstorms uprooted trees and brought down power lines to parts of the Phoenix metropolitan area. About 6,000 SRP customers were without power in the Gilbert area. Dense blowing dust also resulted in very low visibility, delaying flights at Sky Harbor airport.
Dense blowing dust and visibility as low as 50 feet reported between Arizona City and Florence.
Power poles and lines down near Johnson Ranch.
Strong winds from nearby thunderstorms damaged traffic signals in Scottsdale.
Dense blowing dust spread across the East Valley.  One spotter reported less than 200 feet visibility.

Several cities throughout the central portion of Maricopa County had major wind damage as a series of thunderstorms and microbursts moved across the area.  According to the Salt River Project, an estimated 65 power poles were blown down, in parts of Scottsdale, Tempe and Mesa. At one point, about 20,000 customers were without power. Arizona Public Service reported about 8,000 customers were without power. At Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport, the official peak wind gust was 59 mph. However, winds at Williams Gateway Airport gusted to 86 mph and flipped a small twin-engine plane atop another aircraft. In Mesa, 35 schools reported damages due to the storm.  In addition to numerous trees and homes damaged by winds, locally heavy rainfall caused some flooding of streets thoughout the Valley. One of the heaviest amounts was 2.70 inches at Crossroads Park.
Power poles down and roofs damaged due to strong thunderstorm winds.

Strong winds affected parts of Scottsdale, Cave Creek and Carefree. Power lines were knocked down leaving about 16,800 customers without power. The strongest wind recorded at Scottsdale airport was 61 mph.
Power poles down and sheds blown over.
Visibility was reported to be less than a quarter mile across most of Northern Pinal County.
Near zero visibility due to dense blowing dust between Florence and Coolidge.

Power poles downed by high winds which reached as high as 59 mph at Falcon Field. About 16,000 homes were without power at the height of the storm. Dense blowing dust, with zero visibility was reported on the Superstition Freeway.
Microburst winds damaged windows and doors. Camper damaged at a truck stop along Interstate 10.
Near zero visibility at Interstate 10 and Litchfield Road.
100 foot visibility was reported at Picacho Peak State Park due to a wall of dust.

Strong and gusty winds, estimated at 45 to 50 mph caused near zero visibility in a number of locations around the metro Phoenix area. Some power outages were reported, mainly in the West Valley area near Buckeye.
Several road were flooded in the Community of Sun Sites (25 miles south of the City of Willcox).

Several washes near Kolb Road and Golf Links Road were flooded.  At about 1800 MST two boys, ages 11 and 13, were swept down Arcadia wash near Rosemont Boulevard and East Pima Street by flood waters.  The boys were carried about one half mile down the wash before being safely rescued.  Neither of the boys were harmed.
A trained spotter reported 0.62 of an inch of rain in 15 minutes with several streets flooding.  There was street flooding near Moore Road and Rancho Vistoso Boulevard.

A trained spotter in the City of Green Valley reported 1.51 inches of rain in 30 minutes with widespread street flooding.  Another spotter reported 1.20 inches of rain in less than an hour and additional street flooding.
A trained spotter reported 1.50 inches of rainfall in one hour.  This heavy rainfall caused property flooding in the community of Whetstone, with several washes out of their banks.
A trained spotter reported one foot of water across Emily Road in the Community of Avra Valley.
Local law enforcement reported that Whitewater School Road was flooded with a vehicle stuck in the wash.  Additionally, Central Highway and Davis Road were also flooded.
A county official reported that Ward Canyon and Limestone Canyon had several feet of water flow through them between 2245 MST and 2300 MST.  Limestone Canyon had an area of significant road damage.
Spotters estimated winds 55 to 65 mph.
Pilot reported the funnel cloud to the Center Weather Support Unit.
Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rain, with lightning that caused at least one fire.
Observer at Roosevelt reported .88 inches in just 30 minutes. Spotter reported minor flooding in local creeks and streets.
Radar estimated from 3/4 to 1 inch rainfall across mainly rural areas of Southern Gila County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With tropical moisture pouring into Southeast Arizona, several days of rainfall preceded the July 31st event.  With grounds saturated at most locations, the additional rainfall that fell on the 31st had a hard time soaking into the ground and mainly stayed as runoff.  Rivers and washes quickly filled to and over bankfull, flooding homes and businesses as well as nearby roads.  Some roadways were washed away due to the strong flood waters. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of flash flooding throughout the Tucson Metro Area due to saturated grounds and extremely heavy rainfall.  Numerous road were closed due to flooding throughout the entire Metro Area for many hours.  A United States Geological Society stream gage was destroyed by flood waters in Rincon Creek.  Additionally, there were numerous swift water rescues and car stranded in flooded roadways.  It was estimated that nearly 100 vehicles were flooded. Below is a list of some, but not all, of the roads that were closed due to flooding:Alvernon Way south of Sahuarita RoadCamino De La Tierra north of Valencia RoadCamino Loma Alta north of Old Spanish TrailEl Tiro Road west of Cocio RoadFort Lowell Road at the Agua Caleinte WashHartman Lane from Linda Vista Boulevard to Cortaro Farms RoadLa Cholla Boulevard at the Canada Del Oro WashLimberlost Road at the Agua Caleinta WashLinda Vista Boulevard from Hartman Lane to Camino De MananaManville Road between Reservation Road and Avra Valley RoadManville Road west of Sandario Road east of Reservation RoadOld Spanish Trail at Thunderhead Ranch Road east of Colossal CaveOverton Road at the Canada Del Oro WashPump Station Road between Avra Valley Road and El Tiro RoadPump Station Road between Silvebell Road and Avra Valley RoadRiver Road west of Alvernon WaySahuarita Road from Alvernon Way to Houghton RoadSandario Road from Ajo Highway to Manville RoadSilvebell Road west of Cocio RoadSnyder Road between Kolb Road and Sabino Canyon RoadSoliders Trail at the Agua Calienta WashSpeedway Boulevard at Wentworth Road to Tanque Verde Guest RanchSpeedway Boulevard east of Houghton RoadTanque Verde Loop at the Tanque Verde CreekWilds Road in Catalina at the Canada Del Oro Wash

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With tropical moisture pouring into Southeast Arizona, several days of rainfall preceded the July 31st event.  With grounds saturated at most locations, the additional rainfall that fell on the 31st had a hard time soaking into the ground and mainly stayed as runoff.  Rivers and washes quickly filled to and over bankfull, flooding homes and businesses as well as nearby roads.  Some roadways were washed away due to the strong flood waters.  Below is a listing of rainfall amounts for some locations:U OF A CAMPUS 1.17DAVIS-MONTHAN AFB 1.23TUCSON INTERNATIONAL AIRPORT 1.90AVRA VALLEY AIRPARK 1.07SANTA CRUZ RIVER AT INA RD 0.67SANTA CRUZ R/VALENCIA RD 1.26CDO WASH/CORONADO CAMP 1.50DAN SADDLE 1.06SAMANIEGO PEAK 1.26GOLDER RANCH/ORACLE 5SW 0.87ORACLE RANGER STATION 0.24ORACLE RIDGE 0.87DODGE TANK/CATALINA 5E 1.26CHERRY SPRINGS/CATALINA 6E 1.30PIG SPRINGS/ORACLE 5SSW 1.53CATALINA STATE PARK 0.63CDO WASH/SADDLEBROOKE 1.10CDO WASH/GOLDER RANCH RD 0.83CDO WASH/INA RD 0.63ORO VALLEY PUBLIC WORKS 0.94BIG WASH/RANCHO VISTOSO BLVD 1.10WHITE TAIL NEAR PALISADES 1.69SABINO DAM 2.01GREEN MOUNTAIN 0.79FINGER ROCK WASH/SKYLINE DR 0.86SPEEDWAY BLVD/CRAYCROFT RD 1.22KOLB RD/GOLFLINKS RD 1.42RILLITO R/DODGE BLVD 0.51RILLITO R/LA CHOLLA BLVD 0.71ALAMO WASH/GLENN AVE 1.18TANQUE VERDE/TANQUE VERDE RD 1.61TANQUE VERDE/SABINO CANYON RD 1.73VENTANA CANYON/SUNRISE RD 1.38BEAR CANYON RD/KAYENTA DR 1.34AGUA CALIENTE/HOUGHTON RD 2.40SPEEDWAY BLVD/HARRISON RD 1.54BROADWAY BLVD/TANQUE VERDE LOOP 1.61MANNING CAMP/RINCON MOUNTAINS 1.18PANTANO RD/IRVINGTON RD 1.34PANTANO WASH/HOUGHTON RD 1.22SALCIDO PLACE/MESCAL 5N 0.91RANCHO DEL LAGO/VAIL 3NW 0.71PANTANO WASH/VAIL 0.74CIENEGA CREEK/INTERSTATE 10 1.42DAVIDSON CANYON/INTERSTATE 10 0.91HAYSTACK MOUNTAIN/MESCAL 6S 0.75COUNTRY CLUB AND AJO 1.81TEP/IRVINGTON RD 1.06PIMA AIR MUSEUM 1.22PIMA COUNTY FAIR GROUNDS 0.82CORONA DE TUCSON 0.90ELEPHANT HEAD/MADERA CANYON 0.31FLORIDA CANYON/MADERA CANYON 1.02
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rivers running through the Tucson Metro Area flooded on July 31, 2006.  The Rillito River flooded with water over the cement banks near Dodge Boulevard.  Additionally, the Rillito River was over bankfull just east of the Swan Road Bridge.  River Road near La Cholla Road was flooding from the Rillito River.  Sabino Creek was out of its banks and houses were flooded near Sabino Canyon and Bear Canyon.  Below is a listing of some of the damage, but not all, caused by the flooding and an estimate for the cost of repairs:Sabino Canyon Recreation area road and facility damaged, $100,000Forty homes and businesses flooded, $1,200,000One home destroyed due to flooding, $150,000Water main broke near the Mt. Lemmon highway, $20,000Catalina Highway road washed away, $50,000Agricultural irrigation system damaged, $500,000Cement plant flooded, $400,000Gravel pit flooded, $30,000General infrastructure damage, $500,000
A strong thunderstorm wind gust caused a 2 foot diameter, 40 foot tall tree to be snapped in half 5 miles west southwest of the Town of Catalina.
Golf ball sized hail was reported south of Pine.
Nickel sized hail was reported about 10 miles south of Prescott.
One inch diameter hail fell in Prescott Valley.
Flooding was reported on Carrizo Wash about 8 miles north of St Johns. Two homes and a vehicle were flooded in Carrizo Ranch subdivision.  Water was flowing across Highway 191 north of St Johns.
A flash flood covered Highway 61 near milepost 359 about 10 miles southwest of Concho.
A flash flood covered Highway 60 near Vernon.
A flash flood covered the Concho-Snowflake road about 4 miles west of Concho.
Flash flooding caused several wash outs of Indian Route 2 north of Leupp.  People were stranded in their cars because of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell over southern Navajo and eastern Gila counties. Debris flows and running water affected Highway 73 near Canyon Day,  Kinishba turnoff,  Stables Curves,  and Turkey Creek Road.  Several homes in the area were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm wind gust, knocked several trees down and caused power outages in the Town of Elfrida.
Sever hail (.75 inch) was reported in the City of Green Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance over Southwest Arizona enhanced the thunderstorm activity across Southeast Arizona on July 7, 2006.  Many of the storms brought strong winds and heavy rainfall.  The strong winds caused damage at several locations as well as produced several dust storms along major roadways.  A flash flood watch had been issued due to the weak flow aloft and the high precipitable water value over the area.  Additionally, a outflow boundary from additional thunderstorms in Central Arizona moved through Southeast Arizona further enhancing thunderstorm development and severity.The University of Arizona recorded 1.14 inches of rainfall on the July 7thBig Wash at Rancho Vistoso Boulevard in the City of Oro Valley recorded 1.46 inches of rainfall.Speedway Boulevard at Craycroft Road recorded 1.10 inches of rainfall as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported that several washes were flooded in the Town of Clifton.  Additionally, an observer in Clifton reported that 1.98 inches of rain had fallen in a short period of time.
Flash flooding in Lithodendron Wash covered I-40 near mile post 303.
Flooding in and around the Supai Village.  The river through the canyon grew to between 50 and 75 feet wide. None of the bridges around the village were usable.
A large tree was downed due to strong thunderstorm winds on Dragoon Road about 7 miles south of Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high over the four corners region kept Arizona in a moist flow.  This influx of moisture helped to enhance thunderstorm activity on the 18th of July 2006.  Pantano Wash at Houghton Road recorded 0.75 of an inch of rainfall on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Tucson Law Enforcement reported that lightning had started several trees on fire in an east side neighborhood.
A member of the local public reported a large tree downed by a strong thunderstorm wind gust on the southeast side of the Tucson Metro Area.

The local broadcast media reported that strong thunderstorm wind gusts downed several power poles in the Community of Avra Valley.  Additionally, a car window was blown in while driving along Interstate 10 near the Cortaro Road exit in the Town of Marana.
Local law enforcement reported one inch (1.00 inch) hail in the Town of Patagonia.
A trained spotter reported golf ball size hail (1.75 inches) near exit number 40 on Interstate 19.
The ASOS (automated surface observing system) at the Safford International Airport recorded a severe thunderstorm wind gust of 56 knots (64 mph).

Strong winds from thunderstorms in the Tucson Metro Area caused areas of blowing dust with low visibilities.  A trained spotter reported that visibility was as low as 30 feet one mile southwest of Avra Valley Road.  A United States Geological Society employee reported that visibility was as low as 100 feet at the Trico Road bridge over the the Santa Cruz River.
A trained spotter near the Town of Catalina reported that a strong thunderstorm over the are was producing penny size (3/4 inch) hail.
A member of the public reported nickel size hail, 0.55 inch, along Highway 86 east of the Village of Sells.

A trained spotter north of the City of Sierra Vista reported hail between 3/4 of an inch and one inch in diameter which broke several windows in a house.  The house was located near the intersection of State Route 82 and State Route 91.
A trained spotter reported one inch (1.00 inch) hail in the Town of Elfrida.  A member of the public reported 0.88 inch hail, associated with the same severe thunderstorm almost 20 minutes later.

At 1600 MST a trained spotter in the Community of Tubac reported that the washes were running very high and that about 2 inches of rain had fallen in that area.  Again around 1600 MST the Santa Cruz County Flood Control Official reported flooding in the Community of Tumacacori.  The official reported that a box culvert had overtopped near the frontage road of Interstate 19.  A private residence had one foot of water enter the home and the visitor center at the Tumacacori National Monument also had a foot of water flood their property.  Additionally, at 1640 MST the Santa Cruz County Flood Control Official reported that 8 inches of water was flowing over Interstate 19 at the Chavez Siding Road exit.  Two vehicles were reported to be stuck in the flood water at the underpass located near the same exit.
A Sheriff's Department Official reported that a vehicle was stuck in a flooded roadway on Davis Road west of the Community of McNeal.
A trained spotter reported that 2 feet of water was flowing over Route 86.  Route 86 was impassable from mile post 97 to mile post 108.

Numerous roads were closed due to flooding across the Tucson Metro Area.  There was also a report that there were several swift water rescues on the east side of the Tucson Metro Area due to vehicles becoming stuck in flooded roadways.
Houghton Road was closed due to flooding, south of Interstate 10.

Strong thunderstorms across portions of Cochise County produced strong winds that picked up large amounts of dust.  At 1430 MST a trained spotter reported that the visibility was down to 1/4 mile in blowing dust near the Apache Station.  The trained spotter also reported that wind gusts were as strong as 45 mph.
A strong thunderstorm near Huachuca City, Arizona brought 0.88 inch hail to the area.
Very strong thunderstorm winds caused several power poles to be downed in the Village of Sells, along with several trees.

Strong winds from nearby thunderstorms caused blowing dust which reduced visibility at several locations.  A trained spotter 11 miles east northeast of the Town of Bisbee reported visibilities of 40 to 50 yards along Double Adobe Road.  Law enforcement reported zero visibility at the San Simon Port of Entry along Interstate 10.
A strong thunderstorm brought hail to the Town of Patagonia, with sizes of hail ranging from a quarter of an inch to over three quarters of an inch. Hail was covering the ground in some locations.
A strong thunderstorm produced one inch hail 13 miles west of the City of Nogales.

A strong thunderstorm caused power lines to be downed in the Town of Kansas Settlement and small trees to be downed northeast of the Town of Sunsites.  The strong winds were measured to be 60 mph (52 knots) by a trained spotter in the area and were estimated to be near 70 mph (61 knots) by another trained spotter.

A strong thunderstorm on the Southeast side of the Tucson Metro area downed several trees due to strong winds.  The Tucson Fire Department reported trees downed at the intersection of Irvington Road and Kolb Road as well as near Vail Road at Interstate 10.

A thunderstorm over eastern portions of the Tucson Metro area produced strong winds which downed a large (125 foot tall) tree onto an apartment complex on Speedway Boulevard near Kolb Road.  There were no injuries but eight families were evacuated from the apartment complex so that repairs could be made.  Damage was estimated at the time to be about 350,000 dollars.
Several roads were flooded in the City of Douglas.  Lots of ponding of water on streets and street flooding due to heavy rainfall.
Many roads were flooded and closed throughout the Tucson Metro Area. Several roads that cross the Canada Del Oro Wash were closed as well as Mission Road between San Xavier Road and Campus Drive.
Highway 70 was flooded 2 miles west of the Town of Pima.
Also nearly an inch of rain in 20 minutes.

Thunderstorm wind gusts on the south side of the Tucson Metro Area caused an awning to be blown off a home and onto nearby power lines.  Additionally, debris that was picked up by the wind caused damage to an exposed gas line.
Large hail was reported by a trained spotter near the City of Oro Valley.
Law enforcement in Southeast Pinal County reported that visibility was down to near 20 feet near the Town of Eloy due to blowing dust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood stage was reached near the Town of Palominas.  Coronado National Memorial was closed due to debris across the entire park as well as roadway damage.  Parts of the paved road to the Coronado National Memorial were washed away and the unpaved road to Montezuma Pass was impassable, with tons of boulders and other debris piled on the roadway.  A trail to a popular cave area and another much used path were washed out.  Additionally, large trees were wiped out as well as a picnic area.  The Coronado National Memorial's drinking water system was also destroyed by the flooding.
A trained spotter traveling along Interstate 10 near the Town of Picacho reported that visibility was about 200 yards due to blowing dust.

An intense thunderstorm over the south and southeast portion of the Tucson Metro Area brought strong winds that caused several areas of damage.  Four 15 to 20 foot tall trees were downed near Kolb Road and Golf Links Road.  Large trees were also downed  causing roof damage near Wilmont Road and 29th Street.  Additionally, three power poles were snapped along North Larrea Lane near the intersection of Cloud Road and Pantano Road.  About 7,000 customers where without power.
A thunderstorm wind gust over the Tucson Metro Area caused a metal roof to be torn off of a building which in turn sliced through a nearby power line.
A strong thunderstorm wind gust caused power lines to be downed near Speedway Boulevard and Wilmot Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds caused several 5 to 10 foot trees to be downed in the City of Oro Valley.

A county official reported very heavy rainfall which caused west bound Interstate 10 at mile post 300 to be closed due to flooding across the roadway.  Additionally, a law enforcement official reported that washes were flowing 2 to 3 feet deep near the Community of Dragoon.  Law Enforcement officials also reported that two vehicles were submerged in flood waters on Interstate 10 near milepost 299.

Several members of the public along with trained spotters reported numerous streets flooded and several railroad underpasses flooded and closed due to very heavy rainfall.  Additionally, the broadcast media reported a vehicle was trapped in rising water in Arroyo Chico wash.  Also, a car was submerged in water at the Stone Avenue railroad underpass.
Low visibility across a large portion of NW Pinal County.
Trees uprooted near downtown Tempe.
Very low visibility with blowing dust in Arizona City.
Considerable damage to two aircraft at Williams Gateway Airport. A twin engine plane was flipped onto a single engine plane when microburst winds struck the airport.

South Central Arizona, specifically the low Sonoran desert that included the Phoenix metropolitan area, experienced excessive heat for 16 consecutive days, beginning July 9 and ending July 24. Numerous record high temperatures were set during this period. Phoenix Sky Harbor airport had four consecutive days with record highs...118 on the 21st, 116 on the 22nd, and 114 on the 23rd and 24th. The Maricopa County Department of Public Health issued a subsequent report of 50 deaths caused by the excessive heat, 15 deaths were related to the excessive heat, and 10 deaths remained under investigation as of September 2006.
Also near zero visibility in blowing dust.
On Arizona Highway 68.
Near Interstate 40.
A few stones up to ping-pong ball size.
Reported at Meadview.
Dime size hail and wind damage.
Nickel size hail and strong winds.
Moderate street flooding in New Kingman.
Reported on Pearce Ferry Road.
Small shed ripped up and wooden awning destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across Arizona bringing strong and gusty southwest wind.  Wind speeds at the Tucson International Airport topped 30 mph causing dust to spread across the metropolitan area.  An eight car pile-up along Interstate 10 involved four tractor-trailers and four other vehicles.  Three people suffered minor injuries and were taken to local hospitals. The airborne dust also triggered an air-quality advisory. At least six individuals were admitted into a hospital emergency center and treated for respiratory problems.

A low pressure system moved across Arizona bringing strong and gusty southwest wind.  Wind speeds at the Tucson International Airport topped 30 mph causing dust to spread across the metropolitan area.  An eight car pile-up along Interstate 10 involved four tractor-trailers and four other vehicles.  Three people suffered minor injuries and were taken to local hospitals. The airborne dust also triggered an air-quality advisory. At least six individuals were admitted into a hospital emergency center and treated for respiratory problems.

A thunderstorm near Phoenix produced a strong outflow resulting in a dust storm. Visibilities along Interstate 10 dropped to 1/4 mile between Picacho Peak and Red Rock as reported by an off duty National Weather Service Employee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in the the south part of Kachina Village.  Three driveways were washed out and impassable.
Heavy rain caused street flooding to the top of tires in Pinetop-Lakeside.  Roads were blocked by high water and water entered a few homes.

Strong winds tore shingles from roofs, snapped a flag pole and caused other damage as storms moved toward the northwest.  SRP reported a total of about 50 power poles knocked down during the storms of August 21 and August 22.  Combined figures show an estimated 18,000 customers were without power at the height of the storms.
Very low visibility with dense blowing dust reported in Cibola.
Strong winds damaged about 10 bales of hay...with some bales reportedly blown 150 yards.
Several power poles blown down.
Runoff from heavy rain caused the closure of Highway 64 south of Valle (milepost 208).
Water was rushing across a road in White Cone. People were standing on top of cars waiting to be rescued.

The Brins fire started from an abandon camp fire about two miles north of Sedona.  The fire burned northward over Wilson Mountain and down into Oak Creek Canyon. The fire stopped at Highway 89A to the east and Slide Rock State Park to the north. A total of 4,317 acres burned.
Snowflake and Taylor Police Department reported flooding in Cottonwood Wash and Silver Creek along Highway 277.
Remote camera near the Yuma Proving Ground captured a weak, rope-like tornado over open country.
Lightning caused a house fire near Country Club and Brown Road.
Quarter sized hail fell in Parks for about 10 minutes.
Tonto Creek was flowing out of its banks and was blocking access to numerous homes.
Flash flooding closed portions of Highway 73 from Canyon Day west to Highway 60 (near Carrizo).  Flooding was also reported in Canyon Day Flats and Bear Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Highway 191 between Sanders and St Johns. Water was also flowing over Highway 77 between Holbrook and Snowflake.
Water was flowing over Highway 191 between Sanders and St Johns. Water was also flowing over Highway 77 between Holbrook and Snowflake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood in Polacca Wash caused the closure of Highway 87 about 10 miles south of Second Mesa.

Very heavy rainfall...up to 1.61 inches...left many washes and streets flooded in the Wittmann area. Up to 6 inches of water was flowing across U.S. 60 at one point. One half inch of rain fell in Wickenburg in less than an hour.  Small hail accompanied some of the heavy showers and thunderstorms.
Mobile home blown over.
Heavy rainfall resulted in local flooding along U.S. Route 60 at Patton Road.  At one point, 4 to 6 inches of water was flowing across the roadway in Wittmann.

Showers and thunderstorms were scattered across portions of the county, with brief heavy rainfall in localized areas. Hurricane Ileana, spinning in the eastern Pacific, produced impulses that passed through the state. These impulses, together with a very moist airmass, helped trigger these storms.A spotter in East Mesa reported .5 inch of rain in only 15 minutes. An automatic gauge in Queen Creek picked up over 2 inches and a spotter in Wickenburg reported .62 inch of rain in less than one hour. One of the heaviest rainfall rates was reported in East Phoenix. Runoff from these heavy rains led to a rapid rise in the Indian Bend Wash during the day. At one time there was about 4 feet of water. Two motorists had to be rescued from their vehicles. Lightning sparked several fires and caused spotty power and phone outages.
Very heavy rainfall spread across portions of Northwest Pinal County. One spotter west of Casa Grande reported a half inch of rain in only 8 minutes.
Locally heavy rain in portions of western Yuma County.  Rainfall estimates were between 2 and 3 inches in less than an hour.
Law Enforcement reported that two cars were stuck in a wash crossing just north of Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument due to fast moving flood waters.
Several trained spotters in the City of Sierra Vista reported widespread street flooding due to heavy rainfall.
A trained spotter reported that there was three to four inches of water covering the road at the intersection of Highway 80 and Naco Road in the Town of Bisbee.

Heavy rain fell over a large portion of Yavapai County from Prescott to Ash Fork.  A spotter in Chino Valley reported about an inch of rain in just fifteen minutes with a total of over three inches of rain.  Lowland flooding was observed near Paulden.  Water was running over Highway 89 and was closed in several locations between mile posts 324 and 331.

Several roads reported flooded in town after torrential rainfall hit in a short period. One gauge indicated nearly 2 inches in 30 minutes. A wash overflowed its banks, flooding a nearby home with water up to 18 inches deep.
An eighteen year old Northern Arizona University student was struck by lightning while on an athletic field.  He was critically injured.
A thunderstorm produced wind gusts to 60 MPH (estimated) in Black Canyon City.
Penny sized hail fell for about 10 minutes about 10 miles south of Ash Fork.
Quarter sized hail fell in Prescott Valley.
A spotter near the base of El Capitan mountain reported heavy rainfall with about 1/2 inch in only 20 minutes.
Brief heavy rain resulted in minor flooding of Patton Road. One report indicated one to six inches of water covering the roadway.

Strong winds and locally heavy rains swept through much of Southwest Arizona during the night. Wind gusts to 78 mph were clocked at the Yuma Proving Grounds, along with 1.34 inches of rain. Blowing dust was also likely with these strong winds.
Very heavy rainfall associated with thunderstorms and strong winds affected much of Northern La Paz County during the early morning hours.

A Gilbert man and his 9-year-old son drowned Saturday crossing a wash flooded with up to 6 feet of water.  A Pinal county Sherif's Office spokesman said they were on an unmaintained road when they reached the wash about 2:30 pm and entered it.  The current rolled the SUV. Two other occupants escaped the vehicle and clung to a tree until help arrived.

Over two inches of rain led to rapid runoff and considerable flooding across portions of central Maricopa County. One spotter in Phoenix reported 2.33 inches while many locations received between one and two inches. Portions of I-17 near Peoria Ave, and portions of I-10 were flooded. During the evening storm Phoenix Fire made at least one rescue after cars stalled in the deep water.

Power lines and poles downed by very strong winds across portions of the north and west areas of the Valley. Over 10,000 APS customers were without power at the height of the storm.  Luke AFB recorded a peak wind of 56 mph while a spotter in Wittmann had 52 mph gusts along with pea size hail.
Very heavy rainfall resulted in local street flooding and running washes, mainly in North Phoenix. Near 16th Street and Greenway, there was one report of .42 inch of rain in only 10 minutes.

Heavy rain fell over a large area around Flagstaff.  Street flooding was reported from the downtown area to Doney Park.  In the Fort Valley area...three driveways were washed out, water was running over roads, and the normally dry Rio de Flag had 3 feet of flowing water.
Heavy rain was reported in the Tuba City area with water running over roads.
A flash flood in the Hassayampa Wash stranded a vehicle in a low water crossing on Rincon Road north of Wickenburg for several hours.

Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Grand Canyon National Park. There was a two foot rise in the water level in Bright Angel Creek near Roaring Springs between 7 and 715 AM.  Manzanita Creek had a flash flood at 750 AM that blocked the Bright Angel Tail. Hikers had to wait for the water to recede before they could cross.  Bright Angel Creek rose 4 feet in 2 minutes around 815 AM with boulders and debris roaring by. The flooding was over by 900 AM.
Eight large trees,  more than a foot in diameter,  blown down near downtown Wickenburg.
A county official in the City of Nogales reported one inch (1.00) hail falling at the city courthouse complex.
Dime to nickel sized hail fell at the County Fairgrounds in southwest Flagstaff.
Penny to Nickel sized hail fell from Prescott to one mile west of Prescott.
Nickel sized hail was reported in Happy Jack.
Nickel to quarter sized hail was reported in Wilhoit.
Penny sized hail was reported in Highland Pines...about 12 miles southwest of Prescott.
Washes and streams flooded due to the heavy rains. One spotter reported 1.5 inches of rain that resulted in knee-deep water. Considerable debris reported in some streams.
Quarter sized hail was reported about 12 miles northeast of Seligman (15 miles south of Rose Well).
Heavy rain resulted in some flooding of streets in Phoenix. One spotter reported  2.2 inches in only 34 minutes.
Lightning caused a fire to 500 tons of hay on the Salt River Indian Reservation.
Three homes were destroyed and a county bridge was damaged along Arivaipa Creek. One home in the town of Dudleyville was flooded.
Several roads flooded in the town of Cactus Forest, and widespread street flooding in Eloy.
Thunderstorms winds estimated at 50 to 60 MPH blew down power lines in Black Canyon City.
One inch diameter hail fell in Black Canyon City.
Flash flooding in the Washington Park and Beaver Valley areas north of Payson caused the closure of Beaver Flat Road.  The came up 3 to 4 feet in the low water crossings.

Heavy rain fell over the Flagstaff area northward to Fort Valley. The Rio de Flag flowed out of its banks in the Fort Valley area. Water entered a few homes in Fort Valley. Numerous roads, an underpass, and washes flooded in Flagstaff.  A few roads were closed for 2 to 3 hours.
Power lines down across an area estimated to be about a mile long.
Power lines down and some equipment damaged by strong winds.
Car windows broken by hail.
Large hail reported at Desert Mountain.
Heavy rain resulted in some flooding in Arizona City. One spotter reported .62 inch in only 30 minutes.

Severe thunderstorms and very heavy rain spread across most of the East Valley. Power lines and power poles were down, street signs and vehicles were damaged.   Chandler airport recorded peak winds of 57 mph along with dense blowing dust at 6:40 pm. One spotter estimated the strongest winds at 70 mph near University and Brown, in Mesa. Streets and low spots were flooded.
Three people sought shelter under a tree during a thunderstorm.  Lightning stuck the tree. One person was killed and one person as injured. This happened about 8 miles south of McNary.

Law enforcement in the City of Clifton reported a flash flood along Ward Canyon Road.  The flash flooding continued towards the Town of Duncan, where Railroad Wash overtopped Highway 70 along the east side of the town.  Arizona Department of Transportation closed Highway 70 at Railroad Wash due to the flood waters.  One home was flooded in Duncan from the overflow of Railroad Wash.

A county official reported that Highway 86 was closed at the Sikul Himath Wash due to flooding.  The bridge at this wash was overtopped causing large volumes of water to flow across Highway 86.  Additionally, a swift water rescue was performed on Route 19 due to a flooded roadway.
Heavy rainfall within a short time resulted in many streets flooded. Vehicles were reported floating in three to 4 feet of water on a portion of Interstate 17, near Buckeye Road.
Flash flooding caused the closure of east bound Highway 60 about 7 miles east of Show Low. Debris was cleared from the road and it was reopened by 520 PM.

A severe thunderstorm produced very strong wind gusts.  These wind gusts caused significant damage, with eight power poles being downed on Mission Road.  Additionally, several mobile homes were damaged and some were knocked off their foundations on Ajo Way near Mission Road.
Heavy rainfall over portions of the Eastern Pima County caused street flooding where Lambert Lane intersects both 1st Avenue and La Canada Boulevard.  These intersections were closed.

Heavy rainfall in Cochise County caused widespread street flooding in the City of Sierra Vista as well as the Town of Hereford.  State Highway 90 was closed at State Highway 80 due to flowing water and debris.
Heavy rainfall over portions of Northern Greenlee County caused flooded roadways in Hannagan Meadow.
Heavy rain caused at least three rock slides on Highway 89A through Oak Creek Canyon below the Brins Fire (June 2006) area. The Highway was closed until the slides could be cleared.
Numerous trees downed by strong winds.

Numerous streets and fields flooded south of Arizona City after the Santa Cruz Wash was breached upstream of Arizona City. One area that was hit the hardest was Silver Bell Estates. Three structures flooded in the town of Kearny.
Numerous trees reported down throughout Estrella Mountain Park.
Rancher reported 18 power poles down.

Very heavy rain associated with thunderstorms reduced the visibility to as low as 20 yards. Some very impressive rainfall rates occurred with these storms. Actual amounts included 2.8 inches at Maryville Municipal golf course, 2.17 inches at Grand Avenue and 27th Ave, and 2.01 inches at tow automatic stations in Fountain Hills.
Mobile home blown over.
A waterspout formed over the west end of Big Lake (about 15 miles west of Alpine).  The spout lifted water into the air then moved into the forest.
Severe thunderstorm winds...estimated at over 50 mph blew down trees and took down power lines.

Extremely heavy rainfall across the Tucson Metro Area on the morning on September 7th prompted widespread flash flooding.  Numerous roads across the Tucson Metro Area were impassable and closed due to flooding.  Additionally, at least ten vehicles became stuck in different low water crossings throughout the city.  Numerous swift water rescues were performed.  One man was killed when he was swept downstream by flood waters.  The man was first spotted in the Rodeo Wash at the intersection of South Fletcher Avenue and East Irvington Road.  His body was later discovered 15 miles downstream.  Rainfall amounts throughout the Tucson Metro Area ranged from three quarters of an inch to almost two inches.  The Tucson International Airport recorded 1.14 inches of rain.

Law enforcement reported that a truck was stuck in rising water in the Town of Saint David.  A swift water rescue was required to rescue the occupants of the vehicle.  Additionally, Sybil Road in the Town of Saint David was flooded.
A strong thunderstorm over Eastern Pima County produced three quarter inch hail (0.75) near the Town of Corona De Tucson.

A severe thunderstorm over the City of Tucson produced strong winds that caused significant damage.  Roof damage was reported on a home near the intersection of Park Avenue and 36th Street.  Large trees were downed along Ajo Road.  Roof damage was also sustained on six apartments on South 7th Avenue.  Additionally, a roof collapsed on a home at 32nd Street and 7th Avenue.
Strong thunderstorm wind gusts over the City of Tucson caused four power poles to be downed at 36th Street and Forgeus Avenue.
A severe thunderstorm near the City of Sierra Vista produced one inch hail (1.00).
Golf ball size hail was reported in the Town of Patagonia.
One inch hail (1.00) was reported along Highway 191 northeast of the Town of Hannagan Meadow.

A strong (60 mph, 52 knot) thunderstorm wind knocked down five power poles in the Town of Ajo at a sub station causing a widespread power outage.  The power was reported to be out in the Towns of Ajo, Whey, and Lukeville.
A trained weather spotter reported three quarter inch hail (0.75) in the City of Sierra Vista.
Strong thunderstorm wind gusts caused a large billboard and power lines to be downed onto cars near the intersection of 22nd Street and Kolb Road.

The local newspaper reported that three large trees were uprooted in the Town of Green Valley.  One of the large trees fell onto a car, causing damage to the car.  One of the other trees fell onto a building, causing some damage to the building.

A strong thunderstorm over southwest Tucson caused significant damage due to strong winds.  A trained spotter reported that several trees were uprooted at Country Club Road and Valencia Road.  Additionally, the media reported that 7 seven power poles were down along Old Nogales Highway.  Also, 5 trees were reported being uprooted along Aviation Highway at Country Club Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell over southern Navajo and eastern Gila counties. Debris flows and running water affected Highway 73 near Canyon Day,  Kinishba turnoff,  Stables Curves,  and Turkey Creek Road.  Several homes in the area were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell in the Cottonwood area.  Roads washed out and concrete slabs were displaced.  Several homes had six to twelve inches of water in them.
A 45 year old man was struck and killed by lightning about 200 yards off the Wahweap Marina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over portions of northern Arizona on July 29th and 30th. The following is a list of what some of the co-op stations in Coconino and Yavapai Counties recorded in the 24 hour period from 5 AM to 5 AM: Cottonwood 1.50" to 2.30",  Cornville 2.28" to 2.75",   Munds Park 1.65",  and Parks 2.00" to 3.75".

Heavy rain fell over portions of northern Arizona on July 29th and 30th. The following is a list of what some of the co-op stations in Coconino and Yavapai Counties recorded in the 24 hour period from 5 AM to 5 AM: Cottonwood 1.50" to 2.30",  Cornville 2.28" to 2.75",   Munds Park 1.65",  and Parks 2.00" to 3.75".

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood covered I-40 between mile post 217 and 219 near Twin Arrows. Several traffic accidents reported.
Thunderstorm winds brought down trees and branches near 16th Street and Greenway.
A roof was damaged by very strong winds, and heavy rain washed out some dirt roads.  Small hail was also reported.

The intersection of Snyder Road and Catalina Highway was flooded and impassable.  A vehicle became stuck on the intersection and a swift water rescue was performed to rescue the occupants of the vehicle.

Lee Street at the Alamo Wash was closed due to flooding of the roadway.  Water was two feet deep at crest.  Additionally, a car was stalled in the intersection of 5th Street and Sahara Street due to flooding.
Widespread street flooding was reported in the City of Sierra Vista.  Many road were closed and impassable.
Sonoita Wash flooded several roadways near the Town of Rio Rico, making those roads impassable.
The Gu Vo Wash flooded several roadways making them impassable near the Village of Pisinimo.
Heavy rain caused at three debris slides over Highway 89A in Oak Creek Canyon near Sedona. One slide was 100 feet wide and covered the road 4 feet deep. This area was below the Brins Fire scar area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding was reported from Ganado southwest to the Dilkon area. Other areas affected were Cornfields, Cottonwood, Steamboat Canyon, Sunrise Springs, and Greasewood.  Numerous unpaved roads and several bridges were washed out.  Several earthen dams were either overtopped or washed out.  People were moved to temporary shelters.

Widespread flash flooding was reported from Ganado southwest to the Dilkon area. Other areas affected were Cornfields, Cottonwood, Steamboat Canyon, Sunrise Springs, and Greasewood.  Numerous unpaved roads and several bridges were washed out.  Several earthen dams were either overtopped or washed out.  People were moved to temporary shelters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in the City of Green Valley reported that visibility was down to one quarter mile (1/4 mile) due to a dust storm pushing through the area.

Heavy rainfall over Mount Graham, just southwest of the City of Safford, caused roadway flooding along Highway 366.  The Arizona Department of Transportation reported water, rocks and debris over the highway at mile post 121 (Noon Creek) and again at mile post 123 (Wet Canyon).
Charleston Road, between the Town of Tombstone and the Town of Sierra Vista, was flooded and part of the road was washed away.  Damage to the road occurred between mile post 12 and mile post 14.
Davis Road between the Town of Tombstone and the Town of McNeal was flooded and impassable due to heavy rainfall.
Nickel sized hail was reported in east Flagstaff.
Three quarter inch diameter hail was reported in Dewey.
A RAWS 5 miles south of Yarnell recorded a 67 MPH wind gust with from a thunderstorm.
Thunderstorm wind gusts caused carport roof damage in Cordes Junction.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Potato Complex Fire started on June 6th, 2006, and originally consisted of four separate lightning-caused fires ten miles northwest of Heber-Overgaard, Arizona. Named for Potato Creek, the Potato complex burned in Ponderosa Pine, grass understory, Pinyon, and Juniper on the Black Mesa Ranger District of the Apache-Sitgreaves National Forest. There were 374 personnel assigned to this fire including 7 Regular Crews, 2 Camp Crews, 7 Dozers, 13 Engines, 11 Water Tenders, 2 Air Tankers, and a light Helicopter.  The fire burned about 6,300 acres with a total suppression cost of about $3.5 million.
A funnel cloud was seen about 20 miles south of St Johns Airport.
Heavy rain fell in Prescott Valley from mid morning to early afternoon. Between 0.80 and 0.95 inches of rain fell in less than three hours. Local street flooding was reported.
A funnel cloud was reported 15 miles north-northwest of Winslow (10 miles east of Leupp).
Antelope Canyon had 3 to 4 feet of water flowing through it Sunday evening.
One inch diameter hail was reported 7 miles northeast of Snowflake.

Heavy rain caused flooding throughout the Snowflake and Taylor area. Ten to twelve homes were flooded. Water was knee deep in one yard. The Snowflake-Concho road was flooded and impassable.  Highway 77 was also flooded.
A funnel cloud was seen about 10 miles south of the Petrified Forest National Park.
Roads were washed out near the community of Tuweep.

AL Harris Wash and Via Verde Wash overflowed their banks in the City of Nogales near Interstate 19.  Mi Casa Trailer Park and Via Verde Subdivision were flooded.  Mi Casa Trailer Park was evacuated.  Al Harris Wash retention basin was reported to have breached.  Widespread street flooding was also reported throughout the City of Nogales.

Heavy rainfall near the Town of Cascabel and along Cascabel Road caused several locations of the road to be flooded and impassable.  At 19:40 MST (7:40 pm MST) a 1997 Ford flatbed pick-up truck, driving on Cascabel Road near the town of Cascabel (at mile post 23), en route to the Town of St. David encountered flood waters that were deep and moving rapidly across the street.  The vehicle become stuck in the flood water and the two passengers in the vehicle were swept out of the truck and downstream.  Both passengers perished.
Widespread street flooding occurred in the Town of Patagonia and also in the Town of Rio Rico due to heavy rainfall.
A Grand Canyon National Park ranger reported flash flooding on the road toward Tuweep.
A well defined funnel cloud was seen in the Clarkdale/Sycamore Canyon area.
A 75 MPH wind gust was recorded at the Show Low Airport with a thunderstorm.

Severe storms moved through the Tucson Metro Area starting around 7 pm MST (1900 MST).  These storms dropped over an inch of rain at the Tucson International Airport.  Around 8:40 pm MST (2040 MST) reports of flooded roadways became widespread.  A National Weather Service Employee reported roadway flooding at the intersection of Park Avenue and 6th Street.  The broadcast media reported flooding along Sahuarita Road, South of Tucson, with a couple of cars stuck in the flood waters.  Additionally, another five cars became stuck in the flooded intersection of East 22nd Street and South Tyndall Avenue where the water was reported to be over a foot deep and moving very rapidly.  Also, a  newspaper article reported that two homes were flooded due to an over flowing wash at South 2nd Avenue.
One half to three-quarter inch diameter hail covered the ground near Kinnikinick Lake.
Heavy rain caused flash flooding southeast of Tusayan. Water in Red Horse Wash was flowing 2 to 2.5 feet deep over a Forest Service road.
A tornado crossed I-17 about one mile north of Sunset Point Rest Area. This area is mostly grassland and there were no reports of damage.
One and a quarter inch diameter hail was reported seven miles southwest of Flagstaff.
Heavy rain caused flash flooding in the town of Ash Fork. At least a foot of water covered Business I-40 and numerous side streets.
Two to four feet of water was flowing over the Concho-Snowflake Road.
3/4 inch diameter hail was reported 3 miles east of Williams.
1.65 inches of rain fell in Lakeside in just 30 minutes.
A funnel cloud was seen eight to ten miles south of Prescott.
Nickel sized hail fell in east Prescott Valley for five minutes.
Quarter sized hail fell in Humbolt with 60 MPH wind and heavy rain.
Flooding was reported along Highway 87 from about 7 miles south of Pine to the East Verde River.
One and a half inch diameter hail was reported 7 miles south of Ash Fork.
Quarter sized hail was reported 5 miles south of Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At about 11:50 am several spotters and a Highway Patrol officer observed a small funnel cloud moving toward the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very short-lived funnel cloud moved northeastward near the Yuma Proving Ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm over Doney Park produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted for a short while from Doney Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system off the west coast brought deep moisture and strong lift over Arizona. A severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds, heavy rain, and a funnel cloud to Tuba City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Tuba City police officers in different locations saw a funnel cloud over Tuba City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system off the west coast brought deep moisture and strong lift over Arizona. A severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds, heavy rain, and a funnel cloud to Tuba City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a severe thunderstrom caused flash flooding over Highway 160 southwest of Tuba City.  The road was closed until the flood water wend down and the road could be cleard of debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moving across Arizona produced a narrow band of heavy snow for several hours in an upslope area of the Mogollon Rim.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow moved over the Pine/Strawberry area through the night of December 27th into the early morning hours of December 28th. 12 to 15 inches of snow fell by 0800 MST on the morning of December 28th. Lighter snow continued to fall off and on through the day. The town of Strawberry ended up with a storm total of 18 inches. A jackknifed semi truck blocked State Route 87 in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system off the west coast brought deep moisture and strong lift over Arizona. A severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds, heavy rain, and a funnel cloud to Tuba City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 60 to 70 MPH with higher gusts were reported in Tuba City. Roof toops were blown off some houses and windows were blown out of a grocery store. Structure damage and minor injuries from airborne debris were reported from severe straight line winds. Many small branches, and some branches, and a few trees were toppled from the winds. The damage covered a broad path of about two miles wide across Tuba City. Power was knocked out from Page to Tuba City to Kayenta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed along the leading edge of an upper level low pressure approaching Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused power lines to be knocked down and damaged a shed in Kinney Estates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across South-central Arizona and intensified while moving rapidly toward the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.75 inches in diameter was reported across Southern Gila County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across Arizona on Friday Dec 22. The rainfall saturated the lower levels of the atmosphere...and with efficient cooling overnight...widespread dense fog resulted by Saturday morning. |Near zero visibility was reported at Florence...Casa Grande...Apache Junction...Chandler...Gilbert...Queen Creek...Tempe and Phoenix.|At Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport the visibility dropped from 6 miles at 520 am to 3/4 mile at 540 am to zero at 6 am, and the visibility remained less than a half mile until 10 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reported at several locations around the Greater Phoenix area. Zero visibility at both Phoenix Sky Harbor and Chandler Municipal airports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low moved across the Mojave Desert, tapping into unseasonably moist air to produce strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees and power lines, and blew tiles off roofs in Mohave Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low moved across the Mojave Desert, tapping into unseasonably moist air to produce strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding washed out all roads leading into Willow Beach.  Many campers lost all their gear, and were stranded for up to 11 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northern Arizona producing strong wind in Doney Park on the lee of the San Francisco Peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced wind gusts to 60 MPH in Doney Park. Lights were flickering.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm with heavy rain caused flash flooding in Antelope Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Antelope Canyon Tours employee reported a large amount of water flowing through the normally dry slot canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a tornado that tracked nine miles across the Western Mogollon Rim near Bellemont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a tornado that tracked about 9 miles along the Western Mogollon Rim. A NWS storm servey revield extensive tree damage along the 9 mile path. Ten to twenty percent of the ponderosa pine trees along the path were damaged. Very large trees were snapped off at ground level and deposited some distance with no drag marks. This tornado was generally F0. A small section of the damage path exhibited upper-end F1 to lower-end F2 damage. The end of the damage path ended about two miles south-southwest of WFO Flagstaff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow and a strong cold front caused widespread strong thunderstorms with scattered flash flooding across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in north central Navajo County. Roads were closed and washed out in and around the Whipoorwill area. Some of the affected roads included: Indian Routes 4 and 67 one mile south of Whipoorwill, Indian Route 67 in Whipoorwill, Indian 8027, and Rocky Ridge Road near Dinnebito Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Newspaper report of 3/4 inch hail near Recker and Mckellips.
A moist southerly flow and a strong cold front caused widespread strong thunderstorms with scattered flash flooding across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along Lake Shore Drive and the Wahweap Marina between 8:00 AM and 11:00 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow and a strong cold front caused widespread strong thunderstorms with scattered flash flooding across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Apache County Sheriff Department reported flash flooding in North Sanders.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused a rock slide in the Brins fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Brins Fire area caused a rock slide onto Highway 89A through Oak Creek Canyon near Sedona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level cut-off low pressure centered over San Diego, CA pulled sub-tropical moisture northward into Arizona which resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeastern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary tornado caused damage to a trailer, parked at a residence on Avra Valley Road in Marana, AZ, by blowing it onto its side.  Also, a few homes near the intersection of West Avra Valley Road and North Anway Road close to the Town of Marana, AZ experienced minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system ejecting out of Southern California caused scattered thunderstorms to develop over Southeastern Arizona which produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced penny sized hail resulting in damage to skylights of three homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow and a strong cold front caused widespread strong thunderstorms with scattered flash flooding across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Teec Nos Pos and people were evacuated.
